content.text: The central operational challenge of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) training is

: the router learns to route nearly all tokens to a small handful of experts, leaving the rest of the expert sub-networks permanently starved of gradient signal. An unbalanced model wastes the majority of its parameter capacity and frequently performs worse than a much smaller dense model.

This sub-subtopic covers the mechanisms that prevent collapse: the auxiliary load-balancing loss, expert capacity enforcement, alternative balancing strategies, and the diagnostic tools needed to monitor expert utilisation throughout training.

attrs.alt: Expert load distribution: collapsed vs balanced

content.text: The router is a small learned linear projection
whose output is a probability distribution over

experts. During the early stages of training, random initialisation causes some experts to produce slightly better outputs than others for common token types. The softmax gate amplifies these small differences: the better expert receives a higher gate weight, produces a better gradient, improves faster, and then receives an even higher gate weight on the next pass.

dynamic. Once an expert has been selected consistently for thousands of steps, the under-selected experts receive near-zero gradients and their weights stagnate. The final trained model has effectively one or two functional experts and E-2 dead ones — identical in parameter count to a dense model but with the complexity of a sparse routing mechanism.
The standard solution, introduced by Fedus et al. (Switch Transformer, 2022), adds a differentiable auxiliary loss that penalises unequal expert utilisation. Define two quantities per expert
— the fraction of tokens dispatched to expert
in this batch (hard routing count, normalised)
— the mean gate probability assigned to expert
is a small scalar hyperparameter (typically 0.01) and
is the number of experts. The product
is minimised when load is perfectly uniform (each expert receiving 1/E of tokens), because:
If expert i receives many tokens (fᵢ is high), backprop pushes Pᵢ down — reducing the gate probability for that expert
If expert i receives few tokens (fᵢ is low), Pᵢ is also low, so the product is small — no penalty for rarely-selected experts
The product is minimised across all experts simultaneously when fᵢ ≈ Pᵢ ≈ 1/E for all i
normalises the loss so that α has the same meaning regardless of the number of experts. The total training loss is:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def auxiliary_load_balancing_loss(gate_logits, num_experts, top_k, alpha=0.01):
    """
    Switch Transformer auxiliary loss.
    gate_logits: [T, E] — raw logits before softmax
    Returns scalar loss to add to the task loss.
    """
    T, E = gate_logits.shape
    assert E == num_experts

    # Soft gate probabilities (used for P_i) — always differentiable
    gate_probs = F.softmax(gate_logits, dim=-1)   # [T, E]

    # Hard top-K selection (used for f_i) — treated as a constant in backward
    _, top_k_idx = torch.topk(gate_logits, top_k, dim=-1)  # [T, K]

    # One-hot encode top-K selections
    top_k_one_hot = torch.zeros_like(gate_probs)
    top_k_one_hot.scatter_(1, top_k_idx, 1.0 / top_k)  # [T, E], fractional

    # f_i: mean fraction of tokens selecting expert i (hard, detached)
    f = top_k_one_hot.mean(dim=0)   # [E]

    # P_i: mean gate probability for expert i (soft, differentiable)
    P = gate_probs.mean(dim=0)      # [E]

    # Auxiliary loss: alpha * E * sum(f_i * P_i)
    aux_loss = alpha * E * (f * P).sum()
    return aux_loss


class MoELayerWithBalance(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, d_ff, num_experts, top_k, alpha=0.01):
        super().__init__()
        self.experts    = torch.nn.ModuleList([FFN(d_model, d_ff) for _ in range(num_experts)])
        self.router     = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, num_experts, bias=False)
        self.top_k      = top_k
        self.alpha      = alpha
        self.E          = num_experts

    def forward(self, x):
        T, d = x.shape
        logits = self.router(x)                        # [T, E]

        # Compute aux loss before top-K (needs full logits)
        aux = auxiliary_load_balancing_loss(
            logits, self.E, self.top_k, self.alpha)

        # Top-K routing
        top_vals, top_idx = torch.topk(logits, self.top_k, dim=-1)
        gates = F.softmax(top_vals, dim=-1)            # [T, K]

        # Dispatch and accumulate
        output = torch.zeros_like(x)
        for k in range(self.top_k):
            for eid in range(self.E):
                mask = (top_idx[:, k] == eid)
                if mask.any():
                    output[mask] += gates[mask, k:k+1] * self.experts[eid](x[mask])

        return output, aux  # caller sums aux into total loss
The auxiliary loss gradient flows through
(the soft gate probabilities) but NOT through
(the hard token counts, computed via argmax). This is why
is computed from the full softmax over all experts rather than only the top-K gates.

controls how strongly the auxiliary loss pulls expert loads towards uniformity. Too small and the router collapses; too large and the model quality degrades because the router is forced to ignore its learned specialisation.
No load balancing — pure quality signal
Balanced with minimal quality loss (<0.3 ppl)
Router ignores task signal; near-random routing
. In practice, monitor the CV (coefficient of variation) of expert load during training: if CV > 0.5 after 10% of training steps, increase α by 2×.
The auxiliary loss is a soft constraint. For training on large batches where overflow tokens are a real concern, a
is added alongside the auxiliary loss. Define:
is the capacity factor. Any expert that receives more than capacity tokens drops the overflow tokens (their FFN contribution becomes zero for that layer). The capacity limit:
: fixed-size expert input buffers simplify kernel scheduling
: frequent drops indicate a specific expert is overloaded and the aux loss is insufficient
: capacity-dropped tokens still pass through attention and other dense layers

def dispatch_with_capacity(x, gate_indices, gate_weights, num_experts, capacity_factor=1.25):
    """
    Route tokens to experts, respecting capacity limits.
    Returns: outputs [T, d], dropped_mask [T] bool
    """
    T, d = x.shape
    K    = gate_indices.size(1)
    capacity = max(1, int(capacity_factor * T / num_experts))

    output       = torch.zeros_like(x)
    expert_count = torch.zeros(num_experts, dtype=torch.long)
    dropped      = torch.zeros(T, dtype=torch.bool)

    # Process in gate-weight order (highest weight first = prioritise confident routing)
    order = gate_weights[:, 0].argsort(descending=True)

    for t in order.tolist():
        for k in range(K):
            eid = gate_indices[t, k].item()
            if expert_count[eid] < capacity:
                expert_count[eid] += 1
                # (In practice, batch tokens per expert; this illustrates the logic)
            else:
                dropped[t] = True  # token t dropped from expert eid

    return dropped, expert_count
Instead of each token selecting K experts,
flips the assignment: each expert selects the top-
tokens it wants to process. This guarantees perfect load balance by construction — every expert handles exactly
tokens per batch — at the cost of variable token coverage (some tokens may be selected by zero experts).

attrs.latex: \text{Expert Choice: } \text{Top-}K'_t \text{ over tokens for each expert } i

content.text: Expert Choice eliminates the need for an auxiliary loss and the capacity drop mechanism. The trade-off is that tokens not selected by any expert receive no FFN update, which can harm rare-token quality. In practice, a
always-on is added to handle tokens that fall through the routing.

formulated as an optimal transport problem. Tokens are assigned to experts via the Sinkhorn algorithm which finds a doubly-stochastic routing matrix that minimises total routing cost subject to a uniform marginal constraint:

attrs.latex: \min_{P \in \text{Sinkhorn}} \sum_{t,i} c_{t,i} \cdot P_{t,i}, \quad \sum_t P_{t,i} = 1 \;\; \forall i, \quad \sum_i P_{t,i} = 1 \;\; \forall t

content.text: This guarantees perfect expert utilisation but adds
communication overhead for the Sinkhorn iterations and is rarely used in practice.
experts. The shared experts handle high-frequency tokens, absorbing the router's tendency to concentrate load — while the routed experts specialise without collapse pressure:
This architecture reports better load balance than pure-routed MoE at the same total compute, because the shared experts pre-absorb token mass that would otherwise trigger collapse.
Load balance diagnostics should be logged at every checkpoint. The key metrics are:

import torch
import numpy as np

class LoadBalanceMonitor:
    def __init__(self, num_experts, log_interval=100):
        self.E            = num_experts
        self.log_interval = log_interval
        self.step         = 0
        self.f_history    = []   # [steps, E]
        self.drop_history = []   # [steps]
        self.aux_history  = []   # [steps]

    def record(self, f, dropped_fraction, aux_loss_value):
        """Call after every forward pass."""
        self.f_history.append(f.detach().cpu().numpy())
        self.drop_history.append(dropped_fraction)
        self.aux_history.append(aux_loss_value)
        self.step += 1

        if self.step % self.log_interval == 0:
            self._log()

    def _log(self):
        recent_f = np.array(self.f_history[-self.log_interval:])  # [steps, E]
        mean_f   = recent_f.mean(axis=0)   # [E]
        cv       = mean_f.std() / (mean_f.mean() + 1e-8)
        max_load = mean_f.max()
        min_load = mean_f.min()
        drop_rate = np.mean(self.drop_history[-self.log_interval:])
        aux_mean  = np.mean(self.aux_history[-self.log_interval:])

        print(f"Step {self.step:>6d} | CV={cv:.3f} | "
              f"max_load={max_load:.3f} | min_load={min_load:.4f} | "
              f"drop={drop_rate*100:.2f}% | L_aux={aux_mean:.4f}")

        if cv > 0.5:
            print(f"  WARNING: high load CV ({cv:.2f}) — consider increasing alpha")
        if drop_rate > 0.05:
            print(f"  WARNING: high drop rate ({drop_rate*100:.1f}%) — increase capacity factor")
        if max_load > 4 / self.E:
            print(f"  WARNING: expert overload (max={max_load:.3f} vs target={1/self.E:.3f})")
Practitioners sometimes ask whether forcing balanced load hurts model quality by preventing genuine expert specialisation. The evidence is nuanced:
Mild auxiliary loss (α = 0.01) does not degrade quality
: Switch Transformer shows <0.3 perplexity increase vs no aux loss on C4
: forces routing away from learned specialisation; perplexity increases 1–3 points
: a collapsed model with 2 live experts performs similarly to a dense model of 1/4 the parameter count
Expert Choice achieves balance with less quality loss
: by letting experts select tokens, specialisation develops alongside guaranteed load balance
always use the auxiliary loss with α = 0.01 as a baseline
. It costs almost nothing in quality while preventing the catastrophic degradation of expert collapse. Adjust α upward only if monitoring reveals persistent imbalance after 20% of training steps.
Pitfall 1: Aux loss not connected to the optimiser
The most common mistake is computing the auxiliary loss inside the model forward pass but not including it in the scalar passed to
is computed via softmax and needs gradients to flow into the router weights, the aux loss must be explicitly summed before calling backward:

# WRONG: aux_loss computed but not added
output, aux_loss = moe_layer(x)
task_loss = criterion(output, targets)
task_loss.backward()  # aux_loss gradient never flows — no load balancing effect

# CORRECT: sum both losses before backward
output, aux_loss = moe_layer(x)
task_loss  = criterion(output, targets)
total_loss = task_loss + aux_loss   # aux_loss gradient flows into router weights
total_loss.backward()
Pitfall 2: Gradient accumulation inflates aux loss
micro-steps, the aux loss is computed on each micro-batch and summed. If you divide by
to normalise the task loss, you must apply the same divisor to the aux loss to keep

accum_steps = 8
total_loss  = 0.0
for i, batch in enumerate(micro_batches):
    output, aux = moe_layer(batch["x"])
    task        = criterion(output, batch["y"])
    # Normalise BOTH losses by accum_steps
    loss        = (task + aux) / accum_steps
    loss.backward()  # gradients accumulate in .grad buffers

optimizer.step()
optimizer.zero_grad()
Pitfall 3: Monitoring f_i during eval, not train

Expert utilisation is only meaningful under the same distribution as training. If you measure load balance on the validation set alone, early-stopped checkpoints may appear balanced simply because the eval corpus is smaller and token variety is lower. Always measure on a representative training batch sample.

Expert collapse does not affect all layers equally. In practice, lower layers (close to the embedding) collapse more frequently than upper layers, because lower-layer representations are less semantically differentiated and the router has a harder time distinguishing tokens. Log per-layer utilisation separately rather than averaging across all MoE layers.

def log_per_layer_balance(model, dataloader, num_batches=10):
    layer_cvs = {}
    for layer_idx, layer in enumerate(model.moe_layers):
        f_samples = []

        def hook(module, inp, out):
            logits = out[0] if isinstance(out, tuple) else out
            probs  = logits.softmax(-1)
            f_samples.append(probs.mean(0).detach().cpu())

        handle = layer.router.register_forward_hook(hook)
        for i, batch in enumerate(dataloader):
            if i >= num_batches: break
            with torch.no_grad():
                model(**batch)
        handle.remove()

        mean_f = torch.stack(f_samples).mean(0)
        cv     = mean_f.std() / mean_f.mean()
        layer_cvs[layer_idx] = cv.item()
        print(f"  Layer {layer_idx:2d}: CV={cv:.3f}, max={mean_f.max():.3f}, min={mean_f.min():.4f}")

    return layer_cvs
) clips the global gradient norm across all parameters. In a MoE model, the router weight matrix
can receive very large gradients when the aux loss is first applied at a high
Best practice: apply a tighter clip norm to the router parameters and a separate norm to the expert parameters:

# Separate gradient clipping for router vs experts
router_params = [p for name, p in model.named_parameters() if 'router' in name]
expert_params = [p for name, p in model.named_parameters() if 'router' not in name]

torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(router_params, max_norm=0.5)   # tight clip for router
torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(expert_params, max_norm=1.0)   # standard for experts
If training loss spikes or becomes NaN within the first 1,000 steps, check the router gradient norm first. It is almost always the culprit, and reducing the router clip norm or the initial value of
occurs when the router concentrates tokens in one or two experts via a rich-get-richer feedback loop
to the task loss; gradient flows through
to push gate probabilities toward uniformity
is the standard setting; increase if CV > 0.5 after early training
enforce a hard token cap per expert per batch; overflow tokens pass through the layer unchanged (FFN contribution zeroed)
flips assignment so each expert selects its top tokens — perfect balance but variable token coverage
CV, max load, min load, drop rate, and aux/task loss ratio every checkpoint; alert thresholds: CV > 0.5, drop > 5%
Mild balancing (α = 0.01) does not hurt quality
